one_line_takeaway: RAGCharacter reframes RAG poisoning forensics from 'which passage is suspicious' to 'which exact character span inside prompt-used evidence caused this specific misgeneration,' using budgeted black-box counterfactual masking/replay, and substantially outperforms passage-level and even span-adapted baselines on Char F1/IoU/FPR across two QA corpora, five attack families, and six LLMs -- but only within the authors' own benchmark and as a post-incident forensic tool, not an online defense.
key_finding: On NQ (Table 5), RAGCharacter achieves Char F1/Char IoU/Char FPR of 0.874/0.868/0.116 (AbvDecoding), 0.978/0.970/0.003 (CorpusPoison), 0.860/0.844/0.131 (GASLITE), 0.883/0.874/0.112 (PoisonedRAG-black), 0.903/0.888/0.083 (PoisonedRAG-white) -- versus the best original baseline (RobustRAG) at only 0.094/0.050/0.950 on AbvDecoding and comparable low scores elsewhere. On MS-MARCO (Table 6) RAGCharacter scores 0.752-0.955 Char F1 with Char FPR 0.000-0.233, again far above the original baselines (Char F1 0.056-0.280, Char FPR 0.75-0.97). Against the authors' own stronger hybrid character-level baselines (Table 7, averaged over the 4 open-weight LLMs), RAGCharacter still leads on every attack/dataset combination, e.g., NQ CorpusPoison: 0.975/0.965/0.000 (ours) vs. 0.822/0.810/0.182 (RAGOrigin-Character) vs. 0.723/0.701/0.287 (RAGForensics-Character) vs. 0.513/0.406/0.147 (PPL-Character). CorpusPoison is consistently the easiest attack to localize (explicit lexical insertion) and GASLITE the hardest across all methods and both corpora. Separately, Table 4 shows LLM susceptibility to poisoning varies enormously by model: Gemma/Gemma3/Llama3/Qwen2.5 produce traceable EVT poisoning events 40-95% of the time on NQ, while gpt-5-mini mostly abstains (EVT as low as 0.028-0.104) and gpt-4o-mini falls in between (EVT ~0.32-0.57).
core_contribution: RAGCharacter, a black-box, two-pass forensic framework for post-incident poison traceback in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) that localizes the responsible poisoned evidence at CHARACTER granularity (not just the passage/chunk), given a single observed misgeneration event. Pass-0 runs a normal RAG pipeline while logging a prompt-anchored execution trace (top-K hits, the prompt-used subset H_U, the rendered prompt, the completion, and character offsets mapping chunks to prompt positions). Pass-1 is triggered on a suspicious event and re-enters only that logged trace (never the whole corpus) to localize the guilty span via budgeted counterfactual masking and replay: an answer-anchored fast path, then chunk-level occlusion screening (test up to 5 chunks), sentence-level refinement (up to 5 sentences from up to 50 candidates), fine-grained span proposal/scoring (up to 12 candidates), and a bisection step (up to 6 steps, minimum span length 4 characters) that shrinks the winning region to a minimal causal span. The paper also proposes a character-level evaluation protocol (Char IoU, Char F1, Char FPR) for RAG traceback, since prior traceback/removal work is evaluated only at passage/document granularity.
threat_model: Deployed RAG service = knowledge database D + retriever R + generator G (possibly closed-source, black-box, no gradient/parameter access). Attacker can inject or modify a small number of texts in D (white-box or black-box knowledge of the retriever) to induce attacker-desired outputs for targeted queries when poisoned evidence is retrieved and consumed; poisoned payloads may be overt misinformation or stealthy/obfuscated (plausible typos, human-imperceptible injected text). The traceback system itself is operated by the service provider/operator, has full read/write access to D and can rebuild the index, and has query access to the production retriever/LLM, but no prior knowledge of the attacker's template/trigger/strategy and no gradient or internal-parameter access. Explicitly out of scope: attacks that compromise the retriever/LLM's internal parameters or execution environment, infrastructure-level tampering, and adversarial feedback generation -- the focus is strictly on evidence-side (data-layer) poisoning stored in D. A 'misgeneration' is defined operationally (a flagged suspicious (query, output) pair reported by users/monitoring), not by access to a gold answer.
limitations: (1) 'Ground truth' guilty spans and the event taxonomy (Unknown/Confusion/EVT) are defined by the authors' own operational matching against injected answer strings, not independently verified; the paper is explicit that events are defined by string-alignment with attacker-injected content rather than by a gold answer. (2) Evaluation is confined to the paper's own benchmark (two QA datasets, five specific attack families, the authors' own hybrid baselines built for this comparison) -- the paper repeatedly qualifies claims as holding 'within our benchmark,' so generalization to other corpora/attacks/production systems is untested. (3) Pass-1's search is explicitly budgeted (max 5 chunks, 5 sentences, 12 span candidates, 3 rounds, 6 bisection steps) for black-box query-cost reasons; the paper does not report a systematic cost/latency-vs-accuracy analysis of these budget choices, only that defaults 'were sufficient for most single-span and small multi-span cases.' (4) Performance is markedly worse on MS-MARCO than NQ for all methods including RAGCharacter (e.g., Char F1 drops from 0.978 to 0.955 on CorpusPoison but from 0.874 to 0.752 on AbvDecoding), attributed to noisier/more diverse retrieval, and GASLITE remains the hardest attack throughout -- i.e., the method is not uniformly near-perfect. (5) The framework is forensic/post-hoc only: it requires an already-flagged misgeneration event and prompt-anchored logging infrastructure at generation time (Pass-0); it is explicitly not an online defense or standalone removal/remediation mechanism (stated in Table 1's caption), and answer-correctness recovery is treated as a secondary, not primary, objective. (6) Perplexity computation for the PPL baseline used GPT-2, an older/weaker LM than the target generators, which may understate that baseline's true ceiling.

quantitative_results: NQ, RAGCharacter vs best original baseline on AbvDecoding: Char F1 0.874 vs 0.094 (RobustRAG)
NQ, RAGCharacter on CorpusPoison: Char F1 0.978, Char IoU 0.970, Char FPR 0.003 (near-perfect, easiest attack)
NQ, RAGCharacter on GASLITE (hardest attack): Char F1 0.860, Char IoU 0.844, Char FPR 0.131
MS-MARCO, RAGCharacter on CorpusPoison: Char F1 0.955, Char IoU 0.939, Char FPR 0.000
MS-MARCO, RAGCharacter on AbvDecoding (hardest on this corpus): Char F1 0.752, Char IoU 0.721, Char FPR 0.182

Table 7 hybrid-baseline comparison, NQ CorpusPoison: RAGCharacter 0.975/0.965/0.000 vs RAGOrigin-Character 0.822/0.810/0.182 vs RAGForensics-Character 0.723/0.701/0.287 vs PPL-Character 0.513/0.406/0.147 (Char F1/IoU/FPR)

LLM poisoning susceptibility (EVT rate) on NQ across attacks: Gemma 0.86-0.95, Gemma3 0.82-0.93, Llama3 0.72-0.92, Qwen2.5 0.40-0.86, gpt-4o-mini 0.48-0.57, gpt-5-mini 0.028-0.064 (mostly abstains instead)
baselines_compared: RAGOrigin (retrieval similarity + semantic correlation + generation influence, k-means separation)
PPL-Character (authors' hybrid: event-conditional, span-local PPL + occlusion refinement)
RAGForensics-Character (authors' hybrid: judge LLM extracts verbatim span from RAGForensics-flagged chunk)
RAGOrigin-Character (authors' hybrid: judge LLM extracts span from RAGOrigin-ranked chunk)
